title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when  generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens

input_row.title: Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when  generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens
input_row.description: Use stored access policies (SAP) when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens in Azure to centrally manage permissions, expiration, and revocation settings for resource access. Stored access policies can be applied to blob containers, file shares, queues, and tables.
input_row.rationale: Stored access policies provide centralized control over SAS token access, allowing administrators to update permissions or revoke access. This approach strengthens security by reducing the risk of unauthorized access to storage resources.
input_row.audit: It is not currently possible to retrieve a list of all generated SAS tokens to check if they were associated with a SAP during creation. An SAS token that has been created with a SAP will contain an si parameter that references the stored access policy identifier associated with the SAS, e.g. si=. The si parameter will be absent from an SAS token created without a SAP.
input_row.remediation: Remediate from Azure Portal To create a SAP for a blob container: 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Data storage, click Containers. 4. Click the name of a container. 5. Under Settings, click Access policy. 6. Under Stored access policies, click + Add policy. 7. Enter an Identifier. 8. From the Permissions drop-down, select appropriate permissions for the policy. 9. Set an appropriate Start time for the policy. 10. Set an appropriate Expiry time for the policy. 11. Click OK. 12. Click Save. 13. Repeat steps 1-12 as needed to create SAP. When generating SAS, select a SAP from the Stored access policy drop-down. If SAS have been created without a SAP, the SAS can be revoked by regenerating the storage account access keys: Note: Regenerating access keys can affect any applications or Azure services that are dependent on the storage account key. 1. Go to Storage accounts. 2. Click on a storage account. 3. Under Security + networking, click Access keys. 4. Next to each key, click Rotate key. 5. Click Yes to confirm. 6. Repeat steps 1-5 as needed to revoke SAS.
input_row.impact: There is no cost for creating stored access policies, however there is some administrative overhead involved in managing these policies.
input_row.default_value: By default, stored access policies are not associated with SAS. To use a stored access policy, it must be explicitly created and linked to the SAS at the time of creation.
input_row.additional_information: This recommendation is based on the recommendation Ensure stored access policies (SAP) are used when generating shared access signature (SAS) tokens, from the Common Reference Recommendations > Secrets and Keys > Shared Access Signatures section.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Access the AWS Management Console**: 
   - Navigate to your AWS Management Console and log in.

2. **Go to the S3 Service**:
   - Click on "Services" in the top-left corner and select "S3" from the list of services.

3. **Select a Bucket**: 
   - From the list of your S3 buckets, click on the name of the bucket you want to inspect.

4. **Check Bucket Policy or Access Control List (ACL)**:
   - On the bucket details page, click on the "Permissions" tab.
   - Review the "Bucket Policy" to see if any Shared Access Signature (SAS) tokens are being used. (Note: AWS primarily uses pre-signed URLs rather than SAS tokens for similar functionality).

5. **Inspect the IAM Policies**:
   - Open the “IAM” service from the “Services” menu.
   - Review the permissions policies associated with users and roles that can access this bucket. Check for any associated conditions that might simulate the role of stored access policies (even though AWS doesn't have a direct equivalent to Azure's stored access policies).

6. **Review CloudTrail Logs (if enabled)**:
   - If you have AWS CloudTrail set up, review the logs for any events related to S3 bucket access. This could help identify unauthorized access attempts that might indicate improper use of access configurations.

7. **Expected Secure Configuration**:
   - Ensure that any access granted via policies or IAM roles is specific and limited to only necessary actions.
   - Instead of SAS tokens, assess that S3 appropriately uses pre-signed URLs when temporary access is needed.

8. **Confirm Compliance**:
   - Document whether access configurations follow best practices and make sure to maintain records of any changes made. If there are tokens or access methods identified that do not utilize the AWS equivalents of stored access policy management, note these down for remediation steps. 

Please consider that AWS does not utilize stored access policies in the same manner as Azure, so adjustments should be made to comply with AWS best practices around access management.
